message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  The COVID-19 pandemic unexpectedly stopped the steady life and enhanced environmental quality. To apprehend the transmission of COVID-19 and the improvement of air quality, we have studied air quality indicators (PM
                  <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                  , PM
                  <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                  , AQI, and NO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  ), CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  emission, and climate variables (temperature, relative humidity, rainfall, and wind velocity) in the extremely polluted and densely populated Southeast Asian megacity Dhaka, Bangladesh from March to June 2020. The Kendall and Spearman correlations were chosen to test the connotation of air quality and climate variables with COVID-19 morbidity and mortality. The average concentrations of PM
                  <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                  , PM
                  <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                  , and CO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  were 65.0 ± 37.9 and 87.1 ± 52.8 µm m
                  <jats:sub>-3</jats:sub>
                  , and 427 ± 11.8 ppm, respectively. The average PM
                  <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                  and PM
                  <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                  drastically reduced up to 62% during COVID-19 lockdown in Dhaka comparing with March 2020 (before lockdown). Comparing with the same period in 2019, PM
                  <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                  reduced up to 33.5%. The average NO
                  <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                  concentration was 35.0 µmol m
                  <jats:sub>-2</jats:sub>
                  during the lockdown period in April, whereas 175.0 µmol m
                  <jats:sub>-2</jats:sub>
                  during March (before lockdown). A significant correlation was observed between COVID-19 cases and air quality indicators. A strong correlation was obtained between climate variables and the total number of COVID-19 morbidity and mortality representing a favorable condition for spreading the virus. Our study will be very expedient for policymakers to establish a mechanism for air pollution mitigation based on scientific substantiation, and also will be an essential reference for the advance research to improve urban air quality and the transmission of the SARS-CoV-2 virus in the tropical nations.
                </jats:p>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Graphical Abstract</jats:title>
                  <jats:fig id="ufig1" position="float" fig-type="figure" orientation="portrait">
                    <jats:graphic xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink" xlink:href="20193086v2_ufig1" position="float" orientation="portrait"/>
                  </jats:fig>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Highlights</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        COVID-19 lockdown drastically reduced PM
                        <jats:sub>2.5</jats:sub>
                        and PM
                        <jats:sub>10</jats:sub>
                        concentrations up to 62%.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>
                        During lockdown, NO
                        <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                        emission abridged up to 80%, CO
                        <jats:sub>2</jats:sub>
                        emission dropped by 2-4%.
                      </jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Climate variables revealed strong correlation with COVID-19 morbidity and mortality.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>COVID-19 cases and mortality had significant correlation with air quality indicators.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Knowledge from enormous improved air quality will be transmitted to the policy execution.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Impact and Correlation of Air Quality and Climate Variables with COVID-19 Morbidity and Mortality in Dhaka, Bangladesh

reference.unstructured: Hopkins, John. (2020). John Hopkins University of Medicine. Coronavirus Research Center. 20. https://coronavirus.jhu.edu/map.html (Accessed April 17 2020).

IQ Air, COVID-19 Air Quality Report. 2019 coronavirus pandemic lockdowns result in unprecedented reductions in deadly particle pollution (Apr. 22, 2020). https://www2.iqair.com/sites/default/files/documents/REPORT-COVID-19-Impact-on-Air-Quality-in-10-Major-Cities_V6.pdf.

reference.article-title: Temporary reduction in daily global CO2 emissions during the COVID-19 forced confinement

reference.unstructured: WHO Director. (2020a). General’s Remarks at the Media Briefing on 2019-nCoV on 11 February 2020. www.who.int/dg/speeches/detail/who-director-generals-remarks-at-the-media-briefing-on-2019-ncov-on-11-february-2020.

WHO Director. (2020b). General’s Opening Remarks at the Media Briefing on COVID19 on 11 March 2020. www.who.int/dg/speeches/detail/whodirector-general-s-opening-remarks-at-the-media-briefing-on-covid-19-11march-2020.
